data.title: AstraZeneca inks kidney therapy deal worth up to $1.77B with China’s CSPC
data.description:
AstraZeneca (AZN) has reached a partnership worth up to $1.77B with CSPC ‌Pharmaceutical Group (CSPCY) to develop and commercialize treatments for kidney diseases, Reuters reported on Thursday, based on a regulatory filing from the Chinese company.

As part of the deal, the Anglo-Swedish drugmaker will have the option to obtain exclusive global ​rights to develop, manufacture, and commercialize a preclinical small ‌nucleic acid drug candidate. Another option will give the company ex-China rights to a second experimental therapy.

In exchange, CSPC (CHJTF) is entitled to $30M upfront and up to approximately $1.74B in additional payments if it meets certain development and commercial milestones. AstraZeneca (AZN) did not immediately respond to a request seeking additional details on the deal.

Western pharma giants are increasingly turning to China to obtain rights to promising drug candidates, drawing scrutiny from lawmakers on Capitol Hill. Earlier this week, the U.S. House Select Committee ​on China launched a national security investigation into five leading drugmakers over their use of clinical trial sites in China.

data.title: NHTSA closes 2022 Tesla “phantom braking” probe
data.description:
The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration has closed a four-year probe into unexpected deceleration of Tesla’s (TSLA) Model 3 and Model Y vehicles with the model years of 2021 and 2022, having found “no pattern of lane departure or collision risk tied to the events.”

Tesla (TSLA) owners complained of “phantom braking,” in which the vehicles decelerated by 10-20 mph over 1-3 seconds at highway speeds. No collisions tied to the deceleration were identified.

The investigation was triggered by a substantial increase in complaints from 99 at the end of 2021 to 314 by the time NHTSA opened the investigation in February 2022.

The “phantom braking” incidents were separate from an alleged defect in the vehicle’s automatic emergency braking, as the underlying cause for the deceleration was tied to Autopilot, Full Self-Driving, and Traffic-Aware Cruise Control that misread the traffic conditions and initiated an unnecessary stop. 

The agency’s review subsequently determined that Tesla’s complaint and field data aligned with the company’s transition in 2021 from a radar-vision system to vision-only sensing. As a result, phantom braking events declined from 45 in 2024 to just 3 in the first half of 2026.

data.title: Dollar Tree restores share buyback authorization to $2.5B
SK Hynix removing price caps on long-term supply agreements: report
data.description:
SK Hynix (HXSCL) is removing price caps from its Long-Term Agreements, or LTAs, recently signed with its clients, Green Economy News reported, citing industry sources.

Unlike the existing practice of setting price caps for long-term contracts for memory, this structure allows the increase in spot prices to be fully reflected in the contract price if market prices surge due to supply shortages, the report added.

SK Hynix did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Analysts suggest that suppliers' negotiating power is being significantly strengthened as the company, along with rival Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), extends contract periods, the report noted.

Usually, memory chip makers have reduced price volatility by offering price caps to clients in exchange for securing long-term orders. However, SK Hynix has effectively restructured its contracts to maximize profitability during periods of rising prices. The industry views SK Hynix as the only major memory maker currently that does not apply price caps, according to the report.

Long-term contract periods are also extending. As investment in AI servers increases and demand for high-performance memory, including High Bandwidth Memory, or HBM, grows, SK Hynix and Samsung are reportedly extending their long-term supply contracts from the existing one-year level to three to five years. Customers are also showing signs of securing memory supplies in the mid-to-long term, and suppliers appear to be gaining bargaining power, the report added.

American rival Micron Technology (MU) chose a different strategy. In a Strategic Customer Agreement, or SCA, released last month, Micron set the price cap for its existing products at the market price of the second quarter of 2026, the report noted.

The price floor was designed to guarantee profitability higher than in any past business cycle, to make sure that a certain level of profit can be secured even if market conditions deteriorate. The company aimed for both stability and profitability by leaving room for further price increases for the new products, the report noted.

An industry insider noted that as long as investment in AI servers continues to expand, it will be difficult for memory suppliers' bargaining power to weaken easily, adding that even in long-term supply contracts, a trend is emerging where the price structure is shifting in favor of suppliers, going beyond simply securing volume.

On Thursday, SK Hynix said it would invest 100T won (about $64.38B) to build memory chip ‌and packaging plants as part of a South Korean investment drive aimed at semiconductors and AI. Meanwhile, peer Samsung announced plans to invest a combined 140T won (about $90B).

data.title: Red, white, and pricey: Inflation raises the cost of July 4 celebrations
data.description:
87% of Americans plan to celebrate Independence Day this weekend, according to the National Retail Federation, though inflation will add to the cost of cookouts and festivities. Those who have plans for the holiday are forecast to spend over $94 for food items on average (up $2 from last year and $20 from 2020) for a total of $9.4B. More than half of consumers are planning a BBQ or picnic, and 33% of those celebrating expect to purchase additional patriotic items.

Cookout costs: Ground beef prices are up 5.5% from a year ago, per the American Farm Bureau Federation, while the cost of chicken breasts and pork has increased in the mid-single digits. Hamburger buns are also up 7.7% Y/Y, a can of pork and beans is 13.8% higher, and for dessert items, chocolate chip cookies and strawberries are up 6.3% and 12.4%, respectively. Many factors have led to the increase in food costs, like severe drought for America's ranchers and a devastating frost in Florida, as well as high labor costs and increased prices for fuel.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), overall food prices rose by 3.1% across 2025, and that has only continued into 2026. On average, the annual food inflation rate has remained above 3% since January, with the USDA Economic Research Service projecting overall food prices to remain above that level until the end of the year. While that range is already running ahead of target rates, the cumulative effect of several consecutive years of inflation means overall U.S. food prices are around 30% higher than pre-pandemic levels.

Chasing the Mustard Belt: Around 40,000 people are estimated to convene on the corner of Surf and Stillwell avenues in Coney Island to watch the Nathan's Famous (NATH) Fourth of July Hot Dog-Eating Contest. Millions more will watch the ESPN broadcast of the event, which has been formally going on since 1972. Keep an eye on defending men's champion, Joey "Jaws" Chestnut, who ate 70.5 hot dogs during last year's showdown. It might also be the last time Nathan's (NATH) has its own dedicated stock ticker. Smithfield Foods (SFD) agreed to acquire the iconic hot dog brand for $450M in January, with the deal likely to close in the second half of the year.

data.title: InPlay Oil declares $0.09 dividend
data.description:
• InPlay Oil (IPOOF) declares $0.09/share monthly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 10.43%
  • Payable July 31; for shareholders of record July 15; ex-div July 15.
  • See IPOOF Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Biogen projects $164M hit to Q2 results from R&D deals and acquisitions
data.description:
• Biogen (BIIB) disclosed that its GAAP and non-GAAP financials for Q2 2026 will include a roughly $164M pre-tax charge attributed to acquisitions and collaborations completed during the period.
  • As a result, the Alzheimer’s drugmaker projects nearly a $0.95 impact on its Q2 GAAP and non-GAAP net income per diluted share.
  • Citing milestones and transactions that are yet to close, Biogen (BIIB) projected about a $290M-$320M hit to its Q3 2026 GAAP and non-GAAP results due to similar charges. That will amount to a $1.75-$1.95 headwind to Q3 GAAP and non-GAAP net income per diluted share, the company said in a regulatory filing on Wednesday.
  • Biogen’s (BIIB) notable acquisitions during Q2 include its $5.6B buyout deal for eye drug developer Apellis Pharmaceuticals, which was completed in May. In June, the company agreed to acquire private biotechnology firm RayThera for up to $1B.

data.title: Dole expands Nordic footprint with Greenfood fresh produce acquisition
Sodexo S.A. reports Q3 results
data.description:
• Sodexo S.A. press release (SDXAY): Q3
  • Revenue of EUR 6.2B (+1.3% Y/Y).

Outlook

Following a stronger-than-expected third-quarter performance, while maintaining a prudent view of the external environment, Sodexo now expects for Fiscal 2026:

  • Organic revenue growth between +1.2% and +1.5% (previously +0.5% to +1%)
  • Underlying operating profit margin between 3.2% and 3.4%.

data.title: Yara to buy Gulf Coast ammonia plant for $1.3B
data.description:
Yara International (YARIY) (YRAIF) said Thursday it agreed to acquire the Gulf Coast Ammonia production facility in Texas City, Texas, from GCA Holdings LLC, affiliated with Lotus Infrastructure Partners and MB Energy, for $1.3B.

Yara (YARIY) (YRAIF) will own the ammonia plant with an expected nameplate capacity of 1.3M metric tons/year, with Air Products (APD) supplying the industrial gases as part of a long-term supply agreement.

The plant is in commissioning and anticipated to continue ramping up toward full production and stable operations by year-end 2026, with production targeted at above nameplate capacity, the company said.

Yara (YARIY) (YRAIF) said it sees investing in the U.S. as "highly attractive, reinforcing its long-time presence as a reliable provider of crop nutrition solutions and producer of ammonia."

The acquisition follows ​Yara's (YARIY) (YRAIF) recent decision not to proceed ​with its acquisition of ammonia assets ⁠in the Louisiana Clean Energy ​Complex from Air Products (APD).
